segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The Deep Hall received them without ceremony.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin stepped through the threshold and felt the mountain's weight lift -- not gone, just redistributed, traded for the particular stillness of a space that had been holding its breath for centuries. His lungs remembered how to fill.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The ache in his arm didn't disappear, but it quieted, as if even the scale understood that something here predated its urgency.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Then the scale of it arrived. Pillars like old oaks, an obsidian floor that held their torchlight and gave it back doubled. Nine alcoves ringed the walls, each one a different geometry of purpose. The ceiling arched into dark too high to read.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Saints and stone,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Thornik breathed, the words barely a sound.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Nobody told him to be quiet. The Hall already had that covered. There was a hum here -- low, below hearing, felt in the soles of the feet rather than the ears -- and it wasn't welcoming exactly.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: It was the hum of something that had been here long before them and expected to be here long after. They were guests. The kind the house had been built to receive, but guests nonetheless.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: They moved inward, and the dark closed behind them like water.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Dawn came thin and blue through the Deep Hall's lichen glow, turning the carved pillars the color of frost-bitten iron. The air tasted clean here -- cold stone, a thread of old forge-heat sleeping beneath it, and the faint chalk-dry tang of runes that still remembered their purpose.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: They moved like people who had run out of adrenaline and were now running on decision.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Thornik was already at the central table, grandfather's blood-marked journal pinned open under a gear clamp.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Brass goggles perched on his forehead, he traced bas-reliefs with a smith's reverent fingers: dwarves and dragons bent over the same anvil; nine pedestals lifted into place; an undefined chained shape ringed by wards.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Forge Road,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: he murmured, beard-braid ticking against his lip as he measured spacings by thumb-width.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Here. Vent shafts run parallel to it -- old draught chimneys for the caldera. If anything still breathes down here, it breathes along those lines."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He ran his palm along the central panel, tracing figures half his height carved into basalt that had been polished until it forgot it was stone.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "These are the old families. Deepstone, Ironhewn, Blackvein -- the three founding clans who answered when the Concord called."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: His voice had gone somewhere Caelin hadn't heard from him before: reverent and furious at the same time.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "My grandfather tried to tell the elders about this for thirty years. They called him cracked. Said he'd spent too long breathing forge-fumes and reading dead men's letters."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Thornik tapped the journal.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "He wrote that he'd stood in a hall like this once, before the tunnels collapsed. Nobody believed him. He died with the journal under his pillow and his reputation in the privy."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The dwarf's jaw worked once.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Well, Granddad. You were right about the bloody hall."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Serana chalked a sigil on the stone just inside the threshold: nine quick strokes, intersecting arcs that looked wrong until they clicked into simple harmony. The chalk squeaked; the symbol caught the lichen-light and held it, no divine sheen, just stubborn geometry.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Secular ward,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: she said when Caelin looked a question at her.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Old templar trick. No god required -- only ratios and repetition."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She dusted her hands, leaving white on steel-callused palms.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "It won't stop a determined thing. But it notices what crosses."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Elowen lowered herself beside the mark and pressed two fingers to the stone. The vine-ink along her arms pulsed a steady emerald with new threads of silver braided through -- the Deep Hall's abjurations still singing inside her since she'd touched the shields yesterday.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "It listens back,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: she said quietly.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Like a skin wound scabbing -- the Weave remembers the shape of whole. These lines help it hold."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Her smile was small, an apology to the mountain.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "For a time."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Across the table, Caelin worked with his right hand open over a dented tin cup. The scale in his forearm sat at coal-glow, ember-motes drifting slow beneath obsidian like fireflies behind tinted glass.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He called a tight needle of heat -- less than a candle -- and warmed the cup just enough to steam the water without boil. A second thread of focus: a bent pack-buckle on Serana's strap, heat-softened, thumbed true, cooled with a breath.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The filaments under his skin tugged, then settled. Pain: there, but manageable. Like wearing boots a size too small and learning to walk anyway.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Keep the increments small, the cost small,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: he said, mostly to himself. He tried a third exercise: a flat of cold ash on an iron pan, coaxed to lift in spirals, then allowed to fall in his open palm until it sketched Draconic for breathe in soot. The lattice stung once -- needle-pain, warning accepted.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Nyxara watched, her expression the unreadable calm of someone who'd seen a hundred magics and kept score. The tips of her fingers were darker today, necrotic black like frostbite that had learned a language.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She pulled her sleeves down without comment and leaned on the table as if the stone and its memory pleased her.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Three out of ten,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: she said.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin looked up.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "What?"

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Your control. Three out of ten. Possibly three and a half -- the ash calligraphy was a nice touch, though the Draconic was misspelled."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She tipped her head.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "You're thinking about the fire. Stop thinking about the fire. Think about the air between you and the fire. The fire is the easy part. Any fool can burn things. The space between is where the craft lives."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Durgan stood with his back to a ribbed pillar, shadow lying neatly at his feet, hands loose, eyes far. He kept glancing to the chalk sigil at the threshold and then away, as if looking at a knife and deciding not to pick it up.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Vex was already moving, energy channeled into usefulness. She tested the Hall's margins, checked for pressure plates that didn't exist, and returned with three serviceable torches scavenged from niches high on the wall.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "If the dwarves didn't want anyone to touch a thing,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: she said,

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "they carved it so beautifully you couldn't help yourself. If they wanted you to live, they hid ventilation like it was contraband."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She sniffed.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Smoke goes somewhere. I approve."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Thornik snorted.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Thank you. On behalf of my ancestors and basic fluid dynamics."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: They ate a cold breakfast: hard cheese that sweated fat in the chill, bread dense enough to patch a hole, dried fruit with the ghost of sweetness.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The Deep Hall seemed to approve of quiet -- it made their small noises clearer: the scrape of blade on stone as Serana honed an edge; the soft scuff of Elowen's staff; Caelin's measured breathing as he asked the Ember for one more small thing and paid for it with a pinprick of fire that didn't quite become pain.
